metric_name: Total transactions in 1-hour steady-state benchmark
notes: March 2, 2004; 4,503 medical orders from 162 physicians + 1,624 nursing + 1,284 HL7 = 7,411 total orders

metric_name: Peak transaction rate
notes: Sustained during the measured benchmark period

metric_name: Database server CPU utilization at peak load
notes: Implies significant headroom for even greater peak activity

metric_name: Category 1 (admin log-on/log-out) - avg response time (geometric mean)
notes: Overall avg 1.12s due to doctor log-on downloading 150 patient records; log-on 99th pct = 6.74s

metric_name: Category 2 (enter orders) - avg response time (geometric mean)
notes: Subsecond for all except group-order types; 30-patient add-order avg 11.85s

metric_name: Category 3 (patient administration) - avg response time (geometric mean)
notes: Fully subsecond; highest transaction count category

metric_name: Category 4 (patient display) - avg response time (geometric mean)
notes: Fully subsecond

metric_name: Category 5 (worklists for rounds) - avg response time (geometric mean)
notes: Fully subsecond

metric_name: Transaction types with avg response time >1 second
notes: All 8 involved group patient order activities (batch of multiple orders submitted as one)

metric_name: 30-patient add-order batch transaction average response time
notes: Represents 30 patient orders submitted as one batch; auditor observed 17s on slow Pentium III laptop

metric_name: 12-hour extended test total transactions
notes: Sustained >1,000 transactions/minute with no capacity constraints (memory, disk, CPU)

metric_name: Benchmark simulation scale vs. real hospitals
metric_value: 6,000-bed hospital at 5,000 orders/hour = 2.27x busiest known real hospital (2,200 orders/hour)
notes: Exceeds Memorial Hermann (3,179 beds), NY Presbyterian (2,400 beds), Baragwanath (2,600-3,200 beds)

metric_name: Benchmark transaction type diversity
notes: Grouped into 5 categories; includes physician, nurse, clerk orders plus HL7 interface messages

metric_name: Auditor slow-laptop client test performance
metric_value: Acceptable; response times similar to LoadRunner results on Pentium III 750-MHz 256MB via 11-Mbps wireless
notes: Confirms older, slower client PCs acceptable for SunriseXA 3.3 without impeding user-perceived performance

metric_name: Microsoft Windows/SQL Server platform scalability vs. Unix for enterprise workloads
metric_value: Comparable scalability; capable of handling high-end volume workloads
notes: Aberdeen conclusion citing TPC benchmarks and SAP results; Unisys ES7000 shown on par with Unix-based servers

metric_name: Benchmark goal (response time target)
notes: Goal established by Eclipsys prior to benchmark; 74 of 82 types achieved subsecond; 8 exceeded 1s all due to batch group orders

metric_name: SunriseXA 3.5 scheduled availability
notes: Next major release with additional advanced functionality beyond the 3.3 interim release

metric_name: Viability of SunriseXA 3.3 for largest hospital environments
metric_value: Viable; sufficient throughput and headroom demonstrated
notes: Aberdeen concludes product meets requirements for largest hospital environments envisioned

metric_name: Viability of SunriseXA in large hospital deployments - actual outcome
Benchmark isolation test result
metric_value: No perceptible change in response time or throughput when SUT disconnected from wider network
notes: Confirms no external resources were performing any benchmark workload

metric_name: Doctor log-on transaction avg response time (worst single transaction)
notes: Downloads 150 patient data records (doctor works in group of 5 with 30 patients each); Aberdeen deems acceptable given function
